data.title: TIER lifts GDP forecast to 10.38 percent
data.description:
THIRD ABOVE-10 PREDICTION: The institute said its revision reflects accelerating global AI adoption, but warned that deglobalization is adding to inflationary pressures

Taiwan’s artificial intelligence (AI) boom and record export growth prompted a local think tank to sharply raise its economic outlook, adding to a growing consensus that the economy would expand more than 10 percent this year, despite rising inflation risks.

The Taiwan Institute of Economic Research (TIER, 台經院) yesterday lifted its forecast for GDP growth to 10.38 percent from 7.56 percent in April, a revision of 2.82 percentage points.

The upgrade makes TIER the third major domestic research institute to project growth above 10 percent, following Academia Sinica (中研院) and the Chung-Hua Institution for Economic Research (CIER, 中華經濟研究院).

TIER president Gordon Chang (張建一) said that its revision reflects accelerating global AI adoption, which fuels demand for AI-related hardware.

Major technology companies, including Google, have maintained aggressive capital spending plans, generating a steady flow of orders for Taiwan’s technology supply chain, Chang said.

The export boom is also starting to spread beyond the technology sector, he said, citing gains in household wealth from rising stock markets, vehicle sales and a recovery in outbound tourism that has boosted spending on travel-related services.

Despite the upgraded growth outlook, TIER kept its inflation forecast unchanged at 1.98 percent for the year, below the central bank’s 2 percent target.

However, it warned against underestimating inflation risks, pointing to geopolitical tensions in the Middle East and rising costs driven by AI-related demand.

“Beyond oil price volatility stemming from Middle East conflicts, booming AI demand has tightened supplies of memory chips and passive components, pushing up production costs for consumer electronics,” TIER economist Gordon Sun (孫明德) said, adding that computer prices have climbed as much as 20 percent in a single month.

The broader trend of deglobalization is also adding to inflationary pressures as companies diversify production across multiple locations, Sun said.

Some Taiwanese manufacturers operating in the US face production costs that are four times higher than those in Taiwan, with the additional expenses likely to eventually be passed on to consumers, he said.

TIER urged policymakers to closely monitor energy prices, as Taiwan remains vulnerable to higher import costs for liquefied natural gas and crude oil following a nearly 40 percent rebound in global oil prices from recent lows.

The inflationary impact of the Middle East conflict has so far been limited, with strategic reserves helping stabilize global energy supplies, he said.

However, risks might rise later this year as Europe begins refilling natural gas inventories over the summer, potentially pushing energy prices higher, he said.

At the same time, the rapid expansion of renewable energy has reduced global dependence on Middle Eastern oil and gas, TIER said.

Renewable sources accounted for more than 30 percent of global electricity generation last year, overtaking coal as the world’s largest source of power and helping ease the long-term inflationary impact of regional conflicts, it said.

data.title: Ministry aims to boost offshore wind generation
data.description:
The Ministry of Economic Affairs on Thursday set a target to expand offshore wind capacity by as much as eightfold by 2039, doubling down on the technology to curb reliance on imported fossil fuels despite mounting challenges facing the industry.

The ministry released an updated offshore wind road map targeting 18.3 to 19.9 gigawatts of installed capacity by 2035 and 27.9 gigawatts by 2039. The target for 2035 is 18.4 gigawatts.

Last year saw 3.4 gigawatts of installed capacity, BloombergNEF said.

The plan comes despite headwinds for offshore wind at home and abroad.

Projects have struggled to move forward due to rising supply chain costs, strict localization requirements and tougher environmental approvals, forcing policymakers to retreat from a target last year of having 20 percent of its energy produced by renewable sources.

Still, there is growing pressure to speed up renewable energy rollout as the war in the Middle East disrupts natural gas supplies and pushes up the cost of imported fuel.

The nation also became more reliant on energy imports after shutting its last nuclear reactor last year.

The ministry plans to hold offshore wind project auctions every four years, allocating about 8 gigawatts of capacity in each round, it said in the statement.

Power demand is projected to grow at an average annual rate of 2.5 percent from this year to 2035, driven by the expansion in the semiconductor, artificial intelligence and technology sectors, the ministry said.

data.title: Transcend’s net profit surges 46.5% to a new high
data.description:
SHORTAGE-DRIVEN DEMAND: The company said customers have brought forward shipments to boost their inventories amid robust demand for non-AI memory chips

Transcend Information Inc (創見), the nation’s second-largest memory module supplier, on Thursday said net profit surged 46.5 percent sequentially to a new high last quarter, benefiting from robust demand for memory chips used in artificial intelligence (AI), automation and high-performance computing devices, as well as shortage-driven price hikes.

Net profit soared to NT$11.9 billion (US$367.76 million) in the quarter ended on June 30, from NT$8.12 billion three months earlier, the company said.

On an annual basis, net profit skyrocketed 27-fold from NT$421 million in the second quarter last year.

Gross margin climbed to 77 percent last quarter from 76.38 percent a quarter earlier and 34.36 percent a year earlier.

In the first two quarters of this year, net profit swelled to NT$20.02 billion, compared with NT$794 million in the corresponding period last year, hitting the highest level in the company’s 37-year history.

Earnings per share in the first half rose to NT$46.63 from NT$1.85.

“During the second quarter of this year, memory chip supply continued to be constrained as more manufacturing capacity was allocated for the production of memory chips used in AI applications,” Transcend said. “That has caused a further uptrend in [non-AI] memory chip prices.”

“Customers have brought forward shipments to boost inventory in response to constantly strong demand for enterprise servers, industrial automation and HPC applications,” it said.

The growth momentum was also fueled by the company’s long-standing concentration on supplying memory modules used in embedded applications such as enterprise solid-state-drives, industrial camera modules and medical tablets, casino gaming machines and robot controllers.

Customers buying embedded memory modules tend to be less price sensitive compared with those buying PCs and other consumer electronics, as shipments would be halted entirely without equipping them with memory chips, Transcend said.

Some customers downgraded specifications by shrinking memory chip content to cope with severe supply shortages, it said.

Embedded applications made up 63 percent of the company’s overall revenue last year, while the remaining 37 percent came from consumer segments, it said.

Transcend expects demand for high-density memory modules to be robust in the second half of this year as robots, electric vehicles and edge AI applications such as AI PCs continue gaining traction, it said.

The company said it has inked long-term supply agreements with the world’s major memory chip manufacturers led by Samsung Electronics Co to secure as many DDR4, DDR5 and flash memory chips as they can.

However, it still cannot fully satisfy all of the demand, it added.

Memory chip inventory expanded to NT$18.32 billion at the end of last month from NT$5.18 billion at the end of last year, the company’s financial report showed.

Memory chip suppliers raised prices by 40 to 50 percent sequentially last quarter due to supply constraints, with the uptrend expected to extend into the second half, given unresolved supply-demand imbalance, the company said.

However, the price hikes are expected to be less dramatic than the first half, it added.

data.title: Intel’s revenue forecast beats estimates
data.description:
Intel Corp delivered a revenue forecast that shattered Wall Street estimates as booming data center spending fuels a long-awaited turnaround.

Sales would be US$15.8 billion to US$16.8 billion in the third quarter, the company said on Thursday.

Even the low end of that range would easily clear the US$15.1 billion average analyst estimate.

The outlook underscores Intel’s gains with data center customers, which are clamoring for chips to satisfy demand for artificial intelligence (AI) computing. Sales in that segment soared 59 percent last quarter, more than double the pace of Intel’s overall revenue.

Intel would still need to deliver over the longer run, but the latest results are a good sign, Seaport Group analyst Jay Goldberg said.

“You have the near-term momentum to carry you into the longer-term fundamentals,” Goldberg said in an interview on Bloomberg Television.

Intel chief executive officer Lip-Bu Tan (陳立武), who took the reins last year, is working to position Intel as a key beneficiary of AI spending.

Although Nvidia Corp remains the dominant provider of accelerator chips, which help develop and run AI models, the industry’s broader build-out has fueled demand for a range of semiconductors. That includes Intel’s central processing units (CPU).

In data centers, “the CPU is taking off,” Tan said in an interview. “Demand is outpacing our increasing supply, and so those are good problems to have.”

Intel also is making progress in improving its production, Tan said.

That is helping it fill more orders and increasing the chances that other companies would choose to use Intel for outsourced production.

Tan declined to discuss specific customer deals, beyond saying there are “multiple engagements.”

The company should begin to show progress on that front by early next year, he added.

The Santa Clara, California-based company is boosting its spending on equipment to ensure it can meet demand, for its own chips and products that it manufactures for others.

Intel chief financial officer Dave Zinsner said the company, which had earlier planned to reduce capital spending from a year earlier, is now committed to raising its budget — something it would likely do next year as well.

Spending would amount to about US$20 billion this year, Zinsner said.

In the second quarter, revenue rose 25 percent to US$16.1 billion. Profit was US$0.42 a share, excluding some items.

Analysts had estimated sales of US$14.4 billion and earnings of US$0.21 on average, data compiled by Bloomberg showed.

The company is seen as pivotal to bringing chip manufacturing back to US soil, following decades of it shifting to Asia.

Intel’s gross margin, the percentage of revenue remaining after deducting the cost of production, widened to 40.4 percent last quarter on an adjusted basis. That was up nearly 13 percentage points from a year earlier, but profitability remains well below the level when Intel was at the height of its powers.

In those years, the chipmaker regularly reported margins north of 60 percent.

The Intel Foundry Services division — the company’s factory unit — had sales of US$5.8 billion, up 31 percent. The unit relies almost exclusively on Intel product divisions for orders, although it is seeking outside customers.

The PC chip division had revenue of US$8.9 billion and the data center unit posted sales of US$6.3 billion.

data.title: Largan says construction of new line is on schedule
data.description:
Largan Precision Co (大立光), a major camera lens supplier for Apple Inc’s iPhones, yesterday said it is on track to complete construction by the end of this quarter of a new pilot production line for fiber arrays (FA) used in new chip packaging technologies.

Largan chairman Adam Lin (林恩平) earlier this month told investors that the company is pushing into the copackaged optics (CPO) market by supplying FAs and plans to send samples to a customer for certification this month.

Taichung-based Largan plans to start volume production of FAs in the middle of next year, with initial revenue contributions at the same time, Lin said at the time.

The plan is on schedule, a Largan official told the Taipei Times by telephone yesterday.

Aside from the customer to whom Largan expects to send FA samples this month, the company is also working with several other customers on proof-of-concept projects, Lin said.

The company would continue to automate its FA production using in-house equipment, with manufacturing precision having improved to below 0.3 microns, he said.

Aside from FA products, Largan has also developed a dual-row, 80-channel prism-integrated microlens array (PMLA) for 6.4-terabit optical transmission applications, which it unveiled last month.

Largan is more confident about the prospects for its FA business, while development of its PMLA products is under evaluation, Lin said.

There are two main manufacturing approaches for microlens arrays — semiconductor processes and molded glass — with Largan’s PMLA products primarily based on the latter, he said.

It remains unclear which of the two manufacturing approaches would ultimately offer better precision, he said.

If its CPO-related business proves successful, Largan “would not rule out acquiring additional land to expand its facilities,” Lin said.

In preparation for new business expansions, Largan has been acquiring assets.

The company has clinched three deals last month and this month totaling NT$2.57 billion (US$79.42 million), company filings with the Taiwan Stock Exchange showed.

Largan yesterday said it would spend NT$1.38 billion to acquire a 10,134.4m2 site and a building in Taichung’s Xitun District (西屯區) from Unispace Cold Storage Co (亞泰冷藏股份有限公司) to meet capacity expansion needs.

Largan on Tuesday said in a filing that it would spend NT$561 million to acquire land and buildings in Taichung’s Nantun District (南屯區) to consolidate its facilities and support capacity expansion.

The Nantun acquisition covers about 4,124m2 of land and buildings, with a total floor area of about 8,835m2 from Yong Chuan Feng Enterprise Co (永泉豐企業), Tuesday’s filing showed.

Late last month, Largan said that it would spend NT$628 million to acquire a 4,830m2 site in Nantun from Yoke Industrial Corp (振鋒企業).

Largan has 10 company-owned factories and one leased facility in Taichung, company data showed.

data.title: Long haul
data.description: Qantas staff members take a selfie with an Airbus A350-1000ULR, developed for Qantas’ Project Sunrise, during a media tour at Melbourne International Airport yesterday. Airbus engineered the custom A350-1000ULR to power Project Sunrise, which enables nonstop, 22-hour flights connecting Australia’s east coast directly to cities such as London and New York.

data.title: Taiwan remains on US currency manipulation monitoring list
data.description:
The US has kept Taiwan on its latest list of countries to monitor for possible currency manipulation in a semi-annual report titled Macroeconomic and Foreign Exchange Policies of Major Trading Partners of the United States.

Taiwan is among 10 US trading partners that have been kept on the currency manipulation monitoring list, emphasizing that their "currency practices and macroeconomic policies merit close attention,” the US Department of the Treasury said in a statement yesterday.

The other nine on the list are China, Japan, South Korea, Thailand, Singapore, Vietnam, Germany, Ireland and Switzerland, the department said.

The 10 countries were also included on the monitoring list in the January report.

The report uses three criteria to determine if any of the US’ trading partners should be named as a currency manipulator. The criteria are: having a trade surplus with the US of at least US$15 billion; having a current account surplus of at least 3 percent of GDP; and persistent intervention in the foreign exchange market, shown by net purchases of foreign currency of at least 2 percent of GDP.

When an economy meets only one of the three criteria for two US currency reports in a row, it is removed from the monitoring list. However, if a country meets all three criteria, it can be tagged as a currency manipulator.

In this month’s report, the department said it found no major trading partner met all three criteria for enhanced analysis under the Trade Facilitation and Trade Enforcement Act of 2015 during the four quarters ending December last year.

Taiwan met two of the three indicators: a trade surplus with the US and a current account surplus, the report said.

Taiwan's extremely large current account surplus increased further during the report period, driven primarily by strong global demand for technology products, as did its bilateral trade surplus with the US, the department said.

Taiwan's current account surplus grew to 19.6 percent of GDP last year, up from 14.1 percent in 2024, while its goods and services surplus with the US almost doubled last year, increasing by US$72 billion to US$145 billion, the report said.

Regarding market intervention, Taiwan fell just short of the currency manipulation factor, reporting net purchases of foreign exchange of US$7.7 billion last year, about 0.8 percent of its GDP, the report said.

Taiwan's official disclosures on its foreign exchange net purchases were "broadly consistent with Treasury estimates," it said.

Looking more closely at Taiwan's currency practices, the report said the central bank’s intervention was largely aimed at easing the rapid appreciation of the New Taiwan dollar against the US dollar.

However, the central bank also engaged in smaller net sales of the greenback in some months to reduce upward pressure on the US unit last year, the report added.

data.title: Electronics lift industrial output
data.description:
WIDER BENEFITS: Rising AI adoption started lifting some traditional industries, such as machinery and base metals, even as officials expected factory momentum to moderate

The industrial production index rose 22.95 percent year-on-year to 139.44 last month, lifted mainly by the information technology and electronics sectors, the Ministry of Economic Affairs said yesterday.

Last month’s stronger-than-expected performance was driven by robust demand for artificial intelligence (AI) products, which boosted output in the computer, electronics and optical products; electronic components, including semiconductors; and machinery sectors, Department of Statistics Deputy Director-General Chen Yu-fang (陳玉芳) told a news conference in Taipei.

Output of computer, electronics and optical products rose 45.05 percent year-on-year, and that of electronic components increased 30.37 percent, while production of flat-panel displays fell 19.31 percent, down for a second consecutive month, Chen said.

Flat-panel output is expected to recover through the end of this quarter as international brands front-load orders ahead of the holiday shopping season, she said.

In traditional industries, machinery output rose 17.24 percent for a fourth consecutive month of growth, due to demand for chipmaking and pollution-control equipment and components, Chen said.

Base metal output rose 7.43 percent, supported by demand for electronic components and power batteries, as well as a low comparison base a year earlier, she said.

Chemical material output fell 11.66 percent due to weak demand and maintenance at some production lines, while auto parts output declined 1.72 percent amid a sluggish market, she said.

Overall demand in traditional industries has yet to show a clear recovery, given weak global economic conditions and competition from low-priced Chinese products, Chen said.

However, rising adoption of AI applications has begun to support some traditional sectors, including machinery and base metals, she said.

In the first half, the industrial production index rose 19.82 percent year-on-year, while the manufacturing production index increased 21.24 percent, ministry data showed.

This month, the manufacturing production index is projected to rise 22 to 25.5 percent year-on-year, Chen said, adding that production momentum in the manufacturing sector would moderate in the second half of the year due to a higher comparison base.

However, robust AI demand and launches of new consumer electronics such as AI PCs are expected to continue supporting the momentum, with full-year manufacturing output forecast to maintain double-digit growth, she said.

data.title: Power use rises amid AI activity, keeps index ‘red’
data.description:
GREEN POWER SHIFT: Semiconductor and steel demand stayed strong, while renewable purchases made Taipower sales a weaker gauge of factory activity

Taiwan’s industrial electricity consumption rose again last month, reinforcing signs of solid artificial intelligence (AI)-driven economic activity even as sales by state-owned utility Taiwan Power Co (Taipower, 台電) declined, Taiwan Research Institute (TRI, 台灣綜合研究院) said yesterday.

The institute-compiled Electricity Prosperity Index remained in the “red,” indicating “booming” activity, for the 15th consecutive month last month after high-voltage industrial electricity consumption increased 0.58 percent from a year earlier, it said.

The institute uses a five-color system to gauge Taiwan’s power consumption and economic activity, with “red” indicating “booming,” “yellow-red” signaling warming, “green” representing stable growth, “yellow-blue” signifying a sluggish economy and “blue” implying an economic contraction.

The increase came despite a 2.25 percent decline in Taipower’s high-voltage electricity sales, with sales to manufacturers falling 2.56 percent and those to the service sector slipping 0.76 percent, the institute said.

TRI attributed the divergence to growing corporate purchases of renewable energy as companies increase the share of green power in their energy mix.

As a result, Taipower’s electricity sales no longer fully reflect actual industrial power demand, the institute said.

The semiconductor sector remained the largest consumer of electricity, with power usage rising 8 percent from a year earlier, supported by sustained demand for advanced chip manufacturing, advanced packaging technologies and high-performance computing chips, the institute said.

Electricity consumption in the computer, electronics and optical products sector increased 3.46 percent, driven by robust production of AI servers and networking equipment amid continued investment in AI infrastructure, it said.

The steel industry also benefited from AI-related demand, with electricity consumption climbing 10.18 percent as orders for AI servers, power equipment and public infrastructure projects supported production, it said.

By contrast, most traditional industries continued to face headwinds, the institute said.

While electricity consumption in the machinery and equipment sector edged up 0.05 percent, the chemical materials industry recorded a 9.24 percent decline amid persistent global oversupply and expanded production capacity abroad, it said.

Power consumption in the textile industry also fell 11.46 percent as weak end-market demand and intensifying competition from low-priced Chinese products weighed on output, it said.

Rising electricity consumption among semiconductor manufacturers and resilient export orders suggest that AI-related industries would remain the key engine of Taiwan’s economy, the institute said.

It estimated that Taiwan’s economy expanded 11.1 percent last month and projected second-quarter GDP growth of 11.3 percent.

As AI technologies mature and gain wider adoption, they are expected to accelerate supply-chain upgrades, enhance the competitiveness of Taiwan’s semiconductor sector and sustain economic growth, it said.

data.title: Retail, food, beverage sales reach June record amid recovery of auto market
data.description:
Retail and food and beverage sales last month hit a record for June, benefiting from a recovery in the auto market, new store openings, more promotional campaigns and seasonal demand for cold beverages, data released yesterday by the Ministry of Economic Affairs showed.

Retail sales increased 8 percent from a year earlier to NT$421.3 billion (US$13.06 billion) last month, rising for the fifth consecutive month and beating the ministry’s forecast of a 4 to 7 percent increase.

Sales were driven mainly by increased purchases of cars, motorcycles, auto parts and accessories, which saw significant 19.1 percent year-on-year sales growth, the ministry said in a report.

Department stores, big-box stores and convenience stores, e-commerce and mail-order operators, as well as computer and home appliance outlets also contributed to sales growth during the month, rising from 3.2 to 8.9 percent, the report said.

Retail fuel sales increased 11.9 percent due to rising crude oil prices, it said.

Food and beverage sales rose 4.6 percent year-on-year last month to NT$89.2 billion with across-the-board growth at restaurants, beverage outlets and catering service providers, meeting the ministry’s projection of 4.2 to 7.2 percent growth, the report said.

Underpinned by the strong performance last month, retail sales in the second quarter increased 6.1 percent to NT$1.24 trillion, beating the ministry’s estimates of a 4.7 to 5.7 percent increase, and cumulative sales in the first half of the year climbed 4.1 percent to NT$2.46 trillion, a record for the period and also exceeding the ministry’s projection of 3.4 to 3.9 percent growth, it said.

In the second quarter, food and beverage sales rose 4.7 percent to NT$269.3 billion, in line with the ministry’s 4.5 to 5.5 percent prediction, and from January to last month, sales increased 4.2 percent to NT$553.5 billion, also a record for the same period and in line with the ministry’s 4.1 to 4.6 percent forecast, it added.

Sales in the two sectors this month are expected to continue their growth trend, benefiting from the peak summer travel season, increased demand for cold beverages and business opportunities related to sporting events, the ministry said.

Retail sales this month are expected to grow 8.4 to 11.4 percent to between NT$421.3 billion and NT$432.9 billion, while food and beverage sales would increase 4.7 to 7.7 percent to between NT$90.6 billion and NT$93.2 billion, it said.
